chunk_summary: Severe acute malnutrition requires facility-based management with a phased macronutrient delivery approach, focusing on stabilization and rehabilitation using specific feeding protocols and discharge criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for diagnosing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children.
Learn the phased approach to macronutrient delivery during stabilization and rehabilitation.
Recognize the importance of avoiding aggressive feeding and fluid management in SAM cases.
Identify discharge criteria and follow-up protocols for children recovering from SAM.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition is defined by weight-for-length/height < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm (6–59 months), or bilateral pitting edema.
Initial feeding uses F-75 formula to stabilize metabolism; transition to F-100 or catch-up diet occurs when appetite returns and edema resolves.
Avoid iron supplementation during stabilization unless indicated by national guidelines.
Dehydration in SAM is harder to assess; oral or nasogastric rehydration is preferred over IV fluids.
Discharge criteria include at least 15% weight gain from admission weight, edema resolution, and reliable feeding.
reference_quote: The WHO 2023 wasting guideline updates global recommendations; in India, follow national FBMC and nutrition rehabilitation centre protocols while tracking outcomes.
document_key_topics: Severe acute malnutrition diagnosis and criteria
Phased macronutrient delivery for stabilization and rehabilitation
Feeding protocols and fluid management in SAM
Discharge planning and follow-up care for SAM recovery
